3	Stefan Georgi
RESEARCH	Example - Tax Savings Product
Research
EXAMPLE
(Rob Tidwell) Tax Savings Product
Folks, today I’m going to go through Stefan Georgi’s research module from
his infamous RMBC method. Today, I’m going to use a company that I write
for called Taxbot. Just give you a quick background. The frontend is an app
that automates your driving mileage to essentially guarantee your deductions
when you write off your mileage as business. It’s for small business owners,
self-employed folks. The backend of the business is education-based. We
educate a small business owners and self-employed folks on how to save
money on their taxes. Most of our clients, we can save them around $10,000
a year. The kind of research I’m going to be doing is looking at small business
owners, self-employed folks. People have questions about their accounting
and taxes. There’s the background of the research we’ll be doing today and
we’re going to fall along Stefan’s document here, which makes it pretty easy.
So just a heads up, I have gone through and looked up a few spots, a few
forums that I want to look at just to make it easier and faster for the video. I’ll
show you exactly where I went, what I did and we’re going to go further down
the rabbit hole.
First things first, insights into the demographic. Who is your customers? Now,
since I already know the company, I already know some of this information.
Who is your customer? Americans who are self-employed or in a small
business. How old are they typically? Our target range is typically from 30
to 49 years old, roughly. Then we start digging into the real questions. What
attitudes do they have? Religious, political, social, economic. I can tell you
from what do I already know, they tend to be right-leaning conservative
Republicans. Not necessarily on the extreme edge, but just leaning slightly to
the right as opposed to the left. They think that socialism is for corporations
and capitalism is for the little guy. Basically meaning the system is unfair and
is biased towards large corporations like Amazon, Walmart, stuff like that.
So here’s where we’re going to dive in to these forums here. We’re going to
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

4	Stefan Georgi
RESEARCH	Example - Tax Savings Product
try and find all this info, their attitudes, what are their hopes and dreams,
victories and failures, what outside forces do they believe have prevented
their best life, what are their prejudices and then I’m going to try and sum up
their core beliefs about life, love and family in a couple of sentences.
What we’re going to do is dive into some forums here. This is a forum I found
that I like, it’s called Mr. Money Mustache. It kind of has a cult following; it’s
in the the fire niche. The financial independence, retire early, but I found a lot
of good small business owners stuff in here and I’ll go through and show you
what I’ve found.
This thread is not that great other than it goes to show you, “Warren’s wealth
tax may impact your small business or business planning.” What this tells me
is this is a thread I keep seeing over and over is that most of my clients do
not like left-leaning candidates as far as politics go. They are definitely more
leaning to the right as I said. I’m going to try and copy this, it’s kind of goofy,
and I’m going to paste it right here. Oh, that didn’t work. Anyway, I’ll just type
it out. “Warren’s health tax will cost us a small biz owners,” essentially is what
he was saying anyway.
So I rolled through this already and what they’re saying is you don’t want
Elizabeth Warren if you’re a small business owner or going to be is essentially
what it all boils down to. It’s a theme I keep finding, which is good to know.
When you’re writing for this audience, you want to avoid certain subjects that
trigger your target market and so on and so forth. You know what angles to
take, what emotions to play on, et cetera.
Here’s a good topic I found called, “Does anyone regret taking the risk to
start a business?” Oh boy. So we could use that for what are their victories
and failures. I bet we’ll find a bunch in here. I basically just scrolled through
here real quick and looked for things that jump out at me. I’m not going to
read every line. “You may not find people willing to share their stories.” I
know there’s some in here I’m going to pull out and share with you. “A few in
my time.” “Find paying customers,” there’s a good one. “If that’s your goal.”
“Finally started, we’re the same age. “My ultimate dream is to quit my day job,”
boom. What are their hopes and dreams? Well, we just found a good one, “My
ultimate dream is to quit my day job.”
There’s other good stuff in here I want to pull out. “I can’t imagine regretting

starting this business.” Well, we’ll see. “I think jumping ship and starting
buying a business is terrible advice. It’s highly risky and most businesses will
fail.” There you go. People are worried that starting a business is very risky
and that it’s going to fail. It’s kind of a common belief. I find that most people
either think they’re going to be rich and wildly successful or they think they’re
going to fail miserably; there’s hardly any in-betweens. We should put that
on...let’s see, I’m not going to put it in hopes and dreams. My apologies. What
attitudes do they have that would fit here. Attitude being it’s highly risky and
most businesses fail because most businesses do fail as a fact.
Employee feedback would be helpful. Staff aren’t good at his business. “I have
one business that I started with a friend,” and it didn’t work out. “I do regret it.”
Boom. Common theme, something I see a lot. I’m pulling these things out that
you kind of see a lot over and over. We’ll get to that. Victories and failures, I’m
going to put this under failures here. Failure right here, I have one business
and it didn’t work out. I do regret it. Then we go to the next. They don’t regret
it because it was a learning experience. That’s nice.
No horror story. See, here we go. “Most new businesses fail.” The ones that
do succeed do mediocre financially. Starting to see a common theme here,
just in this one thread. Let’s put that under attitudes they have. Going to clean

look elsewhere.” Well here you go. Well, I would never recommend owning
multiple businesses unless there’s very few people that can pull it off, but
that’s another thread. I’ve seen it’s really common. “You never clock out when
you own your own business.” Let’s put that under, I guess failures would
probably be a good spot. Once again, I’ll come back and format all this like it
doesn’t need to be bold or underlined.
Let’s go a little bit more because it’s actually a pretty big thread and when
you’re looking through the threads, you want to see the ones with the most
responses. That’ll be a good sign that there’s some material. If they have
22 responses as opposed to 3, it’s going to be a better thread. “I don’t see
running a side business as a side gig is risky at all.” Interesting. So there you
have it, but he’s talking about a side gig, not full-time. Put that under beliefs,
attitudes. “I don’t regret starting a business.” “I have never regretted starting
our business.” There you have it, you have both sides. I don’t know if that’s
super value but valuable. So we kind of got both sides of the coin.
Scroll to the top. Here’s a good one, “Your bigger regret will be not trying. It’s

of this.

businesses have no customers,” which is probably the biggest one. Cashflow.

What do we have here? Self-employment taxes. A big one for me. There’s

or S corp, cannot say. Really, we just want other attitudes about it not what is

Is self-employment the way or is it just a better way? “Ran my own business

So basically. to sum up their core beliefs; off of everything we just covered in
our research, most of our folks who hate working for a boss at a corporate job,
they want to be self employed, but know the chances of succeeding are low.
They tend to lean right politically, but still think that capitalism works better
for big corporations while screwing the little guy. Many feel trapped in their
businesses and they have no time for vacations or weekends which is a major
pain point. All seem frustrated by complex tax laws and are generally scared
and confused by taxes and their attorneys, overall. That basically sums it up.
A lot of these guys feel like they are getting screwed over while Amazon is paying
nothing with taxes and Apple and all the other folks, so it stirs up a lot of resentment.
That’s kind of what we got out of this research. It’s important. We got to find out
what emotion they’re feeling. So other existing solutions, what are they already
using? Well, they’re on forums for free help because, of course, they don’t pay.

So what does the market like about existing solutions? They like the ones that
have a different perspective on money and the ones that think outside of the
box. That’s basically all the praise I’ve got that I’ve seen about any of the other
solutions, particularly books in this case.
Very few people are thrilled with their CPAs, unless you have a good one and

does the market dislike about existing solutions? Well, outdated information
because tax laws are always changing. Things about small businesses are
always changing. Confusing and conflicting forums because it’s crazy. It’s a

go back and pay a bunch of back taxes because they got bad advice. That’s
basically what it all comes back to; getting bad advice, overpaying, or in the
worst case, having to pay back deductions, back taxes. Does the market
believe existing solution works? Not usually. Pretty skeptical because most
people have had a bad experience. Either they’ve had a bad experience or they
just really don’t know that there is a different way that they can go about it.
